planting.note: Plant container-grown stock in spring in well-drained, average to fertile, slightly acidic to neutral soil in partial shade to full sun; in hot summer regions (zones 8–11) afternoon shade is essential as the glossy foliage scorches in direct afternoon sun and the plant often goes semi-dormant in extreme heat.

directions: Plant container-grown stock in spring in well-drained, average to fertile, slightly acidic to neutral soil in partial shade to full sun; in hot summer regions (zones 8–11) afternoon shade is essential as the glossy foliage scorches in direct afternoon sun and the plant often goes semi-dormant in extreme heat. Acanthus mollis is the plant whose deeply lobed, glossy leaf shape has been immortalized in the decorative acanthus motif used in Corinthian column capitals, medieval carvings, and ornamental plasterwork for 2,500 years; the plant itself is as bold and magnificent as the art it inspired, with very large (12–18 inch), deeply and precisely lobed, glossy dark-green leaves and dramatic, 3–4 foot spikes of white-and-purple-hooded flowers in late spring and early summer that are architectural and unique. In zone 9–11 gardens it can spread aggressively by root fragments; plant where spread is acceptable or confine with a root barrier. Extremely drought-tolerant once established.

planting.note: Plant container or bare-root stock in spring or fall in well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, average to poor soil in full sun; Japanese flowering quince is tough and adaptable, tolerating clay, drought, and pollution once established — excellent for slopes, hedges, and difficult urban sites.

directions: Plant container or bare-root stock in spring or fall in well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, average to poor soil in full sun; Japanese flowering quince is tough and adaptable, tolerating clay, drought, and pollution once established — excellent for slopes, hedges, and difficult urban sites. Chaenomeles japonica is a compact, thorny, deciduous shrub that blooms in early spring before the leaves expand (often in February–March in mild climates), producing a spectacular display of vivid orange to scarlet-red, five-petaled flowers on bare stems — some of the earliest and most vivid spring color in the garden; the flowers provide critical early nectar for overwintered and emerging queen bumblebees. The hard, fragrant, yellow-green, apple-like fruits that follow in autumn are too astringent to eat raw but make outstanding jelly, quince paste (membrillo), and homemade quince wine with excellent flavor and natural pectin content. More compact and cold-hardy than the larger C. speciosa.

planting.note: Plant balled-and-burlapped or container stock in spring in moist to dry, slightly acidic to neutral, well-drained soil in partial shade to full sun; American hazelnut tolerates a wide range of soil conditions but produces the most nuts in full sun; it spreads by root suckers and should be allowed to form thickets for best wildlife habitat value.

directions: Plant balled-and-burlapped or container stock in spring in moist to dry, slightly acidic to neutral, well-drained soil in partial shade to full sun; American hazelnut tolerates a wide range of soil conditions but produces the most nuts in full sun; it spreads by root suckers and should be allowed to form thickets for best wildlife habitat value. Corylus americana is a vigorous, multi-stemmed native shrub of eastern North American woodlands and thickets, producing small, round hazelnuts similar in flavor to European hazelnuts (C. avellana) enclosed in distinctive, toothed, leaf-like husks from August through October; the nuts are eagerly consumed by squirrels, blue jays, turkeys, and other wildlife. Long yellow-green male catkins appear very early in spring (February–March), providing one of the first pollen sources for emerging native bees, and the rounded, hairy leaves turn bright orange-yellow in fall. It is native, low-maintenance, and excellent for naturalizing into wildlife hedgerows and edible landscaping.

planting.note: Plant container or balled stock in spring in moist, well-drained, slightly acidic, humus-rich soil in partial shade to full sun; Chinese witch hazel dislikes wet, waterlogged soil or heavy clay — ensure adequate drainage; it is the most cold-hardy of the Chinese witch hazels and reliable in zones 5–8.

directions: Plant container or balled stock in spring in moist, well-drained, slightly acidic, humus-rich soil in partial shade to full sun; Chinese witch hazel dislikes wet, waterlogged soil or heavy clay — ensure adequate drainage; it is the most cold-hardy of the Chinese witch hazels and reliable in zones 5–8. Hamamelis mollis is the best of the Chinese witch hazels and one of the finest winter-blooming shrubs for temperate gardens; it produces exceptionally large, softly golden-yellow, strap-petaled flowers with an extraordinarily rich, spicy-sweet fragrance — one of the finest natural perfumes of any flowering plant — on bare stems in January and February when the garden is otherwise dormant. The large, soft, round leaves turn butter-yellow to orange-yellow in fall before dropping; and the plant has a distinctive, broadly spreading, vase-shaped form. The cultivar 'Pallida' (pale sulfur-yellow) and 'Goldcrest' (deep golden yellow with red centers) are among the finest selections.

planting.note: Plant container or balled stock in spring in moist, well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, humus-rich soil in partial shade to full sun; Japanese witch hazel is somewhat smaller and more compact than H. mollis and is more tolerant of neutral soils; it is primarily grown by specialists and collectors and is more significant as the parent of H. x intermedia hybrids than as a garden plant in its own right.

directions: Plant container or balled stock in spring in moist, well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, humus-rich soil in partial shade to full sun; Japanese witch hazel is somewhat smaller and more compact than H. mollis and is more tolerant of neutral soils; it is primarily grown by specialists and collectors and is more significant as the parent of H. x intermedia hybrids than as a garden plant in its own right. Hamamelis japonica is a Japanese mountain shrub and one of the two parent species of the Hamamelis x intermedia hybrid witch hazels (along with H. mollis) that include the popular garden cultivars 'Diane' (red), 'Jelena' (orange), and 'Arnold Promise' (golden yellow); the species itself produces distinctive small, yellow, strap-petaled flowers with dark red-purple calyxes (the calyx forms a colored background behind the petals) in January–February, as well as exceptionally brilliant red, orange, and yellow fall foliage that may surpass H. mollis in autumn color intensity. A collector's plant valued for its genetic and horticultural significance.
